nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs of the western Atlantic and Caribbean, where individuals establish cleaning stations on or beside sea anemones such as Bartholomea annulata (corkscrew anemone), Condylactis gigantea, and Stichodactyla helianthus.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices for shelter and molting
host anemones such as Bartholomea annulata, Condylactis gigantea, or Stichodactyla helianthus
established cleaning-station perches near open swimming lanes
low to moderate flow areas (strong flow risks sump entrainment)

diet.notes: Generally considered easy to feed; readily takes mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, sinking pellets, and gel/flake foods with animal-protein content. In an established reef tank they also pick at detritus and graze parasites and damaged tissue from cooperative fish clients. Most sources recommend supplemental feeding 2–3 times per week so the shrimp does not over-rely on fish skin for nutrition.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, small wrasses)
clownfish (with caution — may bully shrimp away from anemones)
Skunk Cleaner Shrimp and Peppermint Shrimp
Sexy Shrimp and Porcelain Anemone Crab
Cerith, Nassarius, and Bumble Bee snails
host anemones (Bartholomea annulata, Condylactis gigantea, Stichodactyla helianthus)
avoidWith: predatory fish (large hawkfish, dottybacks, lionfish, triggers, large wrasses)
any tankmate large enough to consume a small shrimp

summary: Pederson's Cleaner Shrimp is a tiny, translucent Caribbean species that establishes cleaning stations on host anemones and picks parasites from visiting reef fish. Peaceful and generally considered reef-safe, it is a popular choice for nano and mid-sized reef tanks.
careNotes: Pederson's Cleaner Shrimp are generally considered a beginner-friendly marine invertebrate, though their small adult size — reaching around 2 to 3 cm — means they are vulnerable to predation and must not be housed with hawkfish, dottybacks, lionfish, large wrasses, triggerfish, or other fish capable of swallowing them. They are highly copper-sensitive — avoid all copper-based parasite treatments in the display tank, and acclimate slowly to avoid salinity-shock molt failures. The species is strongly commensal with several Caribbean anemones, most reliably Bartholomea annulata, but also Condylactis gigantea and Stichodactyla helianthus; a host anemone is not required for survival in captivity, but the shrimp's natural cleaning-station behaviour is much more readily expressed with one present. Supplemental feeding 2–3 times per week is recommended so the shrimp does not over-graze healthy fish skin. Calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace-element supplementation supports successful moulting.
breedingNotes: Generally considered difficult in home aquaria. Females reach sexual maturity around 6 months and carry 20–60 small eggs per brood under the abdomen for 14–20 days. The planktonic larval stages are challenging to rear: larvae are tiny, sensitive to flow, and easily lost to filtration. A dedicated low-flow rearing setup with appropriate microplankton-grade food is generally required for any meaningful larval survival.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Designated primary URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/ returned the catalog landing page; the working product page was located via search and used in its place.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on access and could not be cross-checked. Listed for traceability; swapped to Aquarium Breeder and Reef Guide as substitute secondaries.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Primary substitute secondary. Provided detailed water-parameter ranges (22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 7–12), host-anemone associations (Bartholomea annulata, Condylactis gigantea, Stichodactyla helianthus), breeding biology, and compatibility lists.

additional.name: Reef Guide (reefguide.org)
additional.notes: Taxonomic / natural-history cross-check. Confirmed adult size 1.8–2.5 cm, Caribbean distribution, depth range 3–18 m, and commensal-shrimp classification.

additional.name: Reef Jar
additional.notes: Hobbyist care-guide cross-check. Confirmed reef-safe status, 20-gallon recommended minimum, Caribbean/Gulf range, and corkscrew-anemone symbiosis; lifespan estimate (3–5 years) disagrees with Aquarium Breeder (~1.5 years).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Juveniles generally moult weekly; adults moult roughly every 2–3 weeks. Shed exoskeletons should be left in the tank as a calcium source. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace-element levels support reliable moulting; sudden parameter swings are commonly associated with failed moults.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females carry 20–60 eggs per brood for 14–20 days; planktonic larvae are small and easily lost to filtration or sump entrainment. A dedicated low-flow larval-rearing setup with microplankton-grade food is generally required. Sexual maturity is reached around 6 months.
